title: Toxoplasmosis and Cats: Truths and Myths of Pregnancy
description: Do you need to get rid of your cat during pregnancy? Science says NO. We debunk all the myths about toxoplasmosis and cats with data from the WHO, CDC, and published studies. Discover how Toxoplasma gondii is really transmitted, what the real risk is, and what precautions to take without abandoning your cat.

segments.voice: Every year, thousands of cats are abandoned because their owners become pregnant. The reason: fear of toxoplasmosis. But data from the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention shows that the primary source of infection is not your cat, but undercooked meat. Today, we're going to debunk the most damaging myth about cats.

Welcome to CatBrothers. This episode is probably the most important one we've made. Toxoplasmosis has generated an irrational fear of cats that has led to thousands of unnecessary abandonments. We'll use only published science, verifiable data, and official recommendations from the World Health Organization.
segments.visual: cat looking at camera innocent eyes

segments.voice: Let's start with the basics. Toxoplasmosis is an infection caused by the parasite Toxoplasma gondii, discovered in 1908 by researchers Charles Nicolle and Louis Manceaux in Tunisia. It's one of the most successful parasites on the planet. According to CDC data, approximately one-third of the global population has been infected at some point in their lives.

The life cycle of Toxoplasma has two phases. The sexual phase only occurs in cats, which are the definitive hosts. But the asexual phase occurs in virtually any warm-blooded animal, including humans, pigs, sheep, and birds. Cats are part of the cycle, but they're not the only source of infection or the most common one.

Crucial fact: according to a meta-analysis published in the British Medical Journal in 2000, the primary source of toxoplasmosis infection in humans is the consumption of raw or undercooked meat, responsible for thirty to sixty-three percent of cases in Europe. Direct contact with cats accounts for less than six percent of documented infections.
segments.visual: raw meat on cutting board kitchen

segments.voice: How does a cat get infected? Only by hunting and eating infected prey like mice or birds. An indoor cat that eats commercial food has a minimal risk of infection. According to Dr. Patricia Conrad, a parasitologist at the University of California, Davis, a cat that never goes outside and doesn't eat raw meat has a near-zero chance of carrying Toxoplasma.
segments.visual: indoor cat eating from bowl safe

segments.voice: When a cat is infected for the first time, it excretes oocysts in its feces for only one to three weeks in its entire life. After that, it develops immunity and rarely excretes them again. Additionally, oocysts need one to five days to mature in the environment to become infectious. Cleaning the litter box daily eliminates the risk almost entirely.
segments.visual: person cleaning cat litter box gloves

segments.voice: A study by the Complutense University of Madrid published in Parasitology Research analyzed feces from over three hundred domestic cats in Spain and found Toxoplasma oocysts in only 0.6 percent. Less than one percent. Domestic cats are extremely infrequent and temporary excretors of the parasite.

Toxoplasmosis in healthy people is generally asymptomatic or produces symptoms similar to a mild flu. The immune system controls it effectively. The real problem arises in immunocompromised individuals and in primary infection during pregnancy, especially in the first trimester, where it can cause congenital toxoplasmosis in the fetus.

But even during pregnancy, the figures are reassuring. According to the World Health Organization, the incidence of congenital toxoplasmosis varies between one and ten cases per ten thousand live births, depending on the country. In Spain, a study by the Carlos III Health Institute estimated an incidence of two to three cases per ten thousand births.

The Spanish Society of Gynecology and Obstetrics published clear guidelines in 2018: it's not necessary to get rid of the cat during pregnancy. The recommendations are simple: avoid cleaning the litter box or do it with gloves, keep the cat indoors, don't give it raw meat, and wash your hands after handling garden soil.
segments.visual: pregnant woman relaxing cat on lap

segments.voice: The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists agrees. Their 2020 official guidelines state that the risk of contracting toxoplasmosis from a domestic indoor cat is extremely low. They recommend that someone else clean the litter box, but emphasize that there's no medical reason to separate from the cat during pregnancy.

There are other facts that fear hides. Gardening is a significant source of infection. Toxoplasma oocysts can survive in soil for months. A study by the University of Montpellier found viable oocysts in twenty-five percent of garden soil samples. Wearing gloves while gardening is more important than staying away from cats.

Water can also be a source of infection. In 1995, a toxoplasmosis outbreak in Victoria, Canada, affected over a hundred people and was traced back to the contaminated municipal water supply with oocysts. Researcher Brent Dixon published the case in the International Journal for Parasitology, demonstrating that the waterborne risk is real but preventable.

Now, let's talk about something few people mention: the effect of unfounded fear. A study by the University of Guelph in Canada published in Zoonoses and Public Health found that fear of toxoplasmosis is the second most common reason for cat abandonment related to pregnancy, after allergies. Thousands of cats lose their homes due to misinformation.
segments.visual: sad cat in animal shelter cage

segments.voice: Dr. Julie Levy from the University of Florida, a specialist in shelter medicine, estimates that over twenty-five thousand cats are surrendered to shelters annually in the US for pregnancy-related reasons. Many are euthanized. Debunking toxoplasmosis is not just a scientific matter; it's an animal welfare emergency.

What about toxoplasmosis and human behavior? Researcher Jaroslav Flegr from Charles University in Prague has published controversial studies suggesting that Toxoplasma can modify human behavior, increasing risk-taking. However, subsequent meta-analyses published in PLOS ONE in 2019 found much smaller effects than initially suggested.

For cats, toxoplasmosis is rarely a problem. Most infected cats show no symptoms. Kittens and immunocompromised cats may develop pneumonia, eye inflammation, or neurological problems. But according to the American Animal Hospital Association, severe clinical cases are exceptional in well-fed domestic cats.
segments.visual: healthy happy cat playing with toy

segments.voice: Prevention is simple. The CDC summarizes the measures in five points: cook meat to over seventy-four degrees Celsius, wash fruits and vegetables, clean the cat litter box daily with gloves, keep the cat indoors, and wash your hands after handling soil. None of these points include abandoning the cat.

There's a serological test that can determine if you've already had toxoplasmosis. If the IgG is positive and the IgM is negative, it means you had the infection in the past and are immune. In that case, there's no risk during pregnancy. Your gynecologist can request it in a routine blood test.

In France, the toxoplasmosis test is mandatory at the start of pregnancy since 1978. Seronegative women are monitored monthly. Thanks to this program, the incidence of congenital toxoplasmosis decreased by seventy percent over three decades, according to data from the French National Institute of Health and Medical Research.

The cat can also be tested. A veterinarian can perform a serological test to determine if your cat has had contact with Toxoplasma. If it's seropositive, it probably already excreted oocysts in the past and is immune, with minimal risk of excreting them again. If it's seronegative, keeping it indoors will eliminate the risk.

One last fact worth considering. Countries with higher consumption of raw meat, like France, have the highest rates of human toxoplasmosis. Countries where meat is cooked well have low rates. If the primary cause were cats, the rates would be similar in all countries with similar feline populations. The data doesn't lie.

Science is clear: you don't need to get rid of your cat if you're pregnant. You need to cook meat well, wash fruits and vegetables, use gloves for the litter box and gardening, and keep your cat indoors. These simple measures reduce the risk to almost zero. Your cat and your baby can coexist perfectly.
segments.visual: happy family baby and cat together

segments.voice: If this documentary can save even one cat from abandonment, it will have been worth it. Share this video with every pregnant woman you know. Subscribe to CatBrothers and turn on the bell. Next week, we'll teach you how to make your indoor cat happy with scientific environmental enrichment. Share now.
segments.visual: happy cat waving paw at camera
